_comment: Each topic's quiz is the MEASUREMENT INSTRUMENT. It is fixed in advance and never generated by the LLM. At least 3 of the 5 questions in every topic are TRAP questions: a student who still holds the misconception answers them wrong, a student who genuinely understands answers them right. The AI student takes this quiz alone and its score IS the user's teaching score. Grading is plain Python against 'correct' (0-based index) in app.py.

recursion.misconception: Honestly? Recursion is just a fancy loop with extra steps. A function calls itself, it repeats, same as a while loop. That 'base case' thing everyone writes is just decoration — the loop would stop on its own eventually anyway.
recursion.why_its_sticky: Recursion and iteration both 'repeat', so learners map recursion onto the loop they already understand and treat the base case as optional boilerplate.

quiz.q: What happens if a recursive function can never reach a base case?
options: It runs once and returns a default value
It keeps calling itself until the program crashes with a stack overflow
The compiler automatically inserts a stopping condition
It behaves like a normal loop and stops on its own

quiz.q: Why does a correct recursive function eventually stop?
options: Because any repeated process stops after enough repetitions
Because each call moves toward a base case that returns without recursing
Because the computer always runs out of memory first
Because recursion is capped at a fixed number of calls

quiz.q: In factorial(n) = n * factorial(n - 1), what actually makes it terminate?
options: Nothing special — like a loop it just ends
The base case factorial(0) = 1, which stops the chain of calls
The multiplication operator
n eventually becoming a negative number
reuses the exact same variables as the previous call, like a loop iteration
gets its own separate copy of parameters and locals on the call stack
overwrites the caller's variables directly
a function that calls itself to solve smaller versions of the same problem
a special piece of for-loop syntax
a trick that always runs faster than a loop

pointers vs values.misconception: When I pass a variable into a function, the function is working on my actual variable, right? A copy and the original are basically the same thing — so if the function changes the parameter, my original variable changes too. That's the whole point of passing it in.
pointers vs values.why_its_sticky: In everyday language 'giving someone your number' and 'giving them a copy of your number' feel identical, so learners assume a passed variable and the caller's variable are one and the same.

quiz.q: A function takes an int x by value and runs x = x + 1. You call it with a variable a whose value is 5. Afterward, a is:
options: 6, because the function changed it
5, because the function only modified its own copy
undefined until you read it again
The function receives a copy; changes inside do not affect the caller's variable
The function receives the caller's actual variable and can change it
The value is shared live between caller and function

quiz.q: In C, to let a function modify the caller's variable, you must:
options: just pass the variable normally — that already modifies it
pass a pointer (the address of the variable)
declare the variable as global first, there is no other way

quiz.q: Two int variables: b = a (a is 5). You then set b = 99. What is a?
options: Still 5 — b got its own copy of the value
99, because b and a are the same variable now
0, the assignment resets a
a private copy of some value
the memory address of another variable
two values packed together

binary numbers.misconception: Binary is basically decimal but you're only allowed to write 0s and 1s. Each position means the same as it does in normal numbers — so binary '10' is ten, '100' is a hundred. You just skip the digits 2 through 9.
binary numbers.why_its_sticky: Learners already own a rock-solid base-10 model where every place is a power of ten, and they transplant that place-value habit onto binary instead of switching to powers of two.

quiz.q: Reading right to left, the place values in binary are:
How many different values can 3 binary digits represent?
